item.content:encoded:
Fascinating.

As a side note, I was about to comment about the weird misspelling of Samson's name in Historical Incident A… and then I realized that's how his Hebrew name is pronounced (which is "שמשון", if you're interested). Great job me, Hebrew is literally your native tongue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: religion-based SCPs are fascinating when done well, and this one sure was! +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your changes are pretty good! Have my upvote :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might be a cultural thing. I'll say that one of the oldest lines about Jewish culture is "two Jews, three opinions," and to be honest, there is absoluterly no way that you'd have several of us in a room and not break out into regular arguments. Without getting political, I'll just use Bernie Sanders as an example — his method of speech is perfectly normal to me

item.title: Good Job!
item.content:encoded: This is great! Just what I like in an article these days. Stay healthy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah ok, the sewage part makes more sense now.

The reason I suggested the changes to the containment procedures is there doesn't seem to be any anomalous properties that effect arguments prior to an event being triggered, so people aware of this effects should be able to keep a debate calm and civil without the use of an outside mediator. I could see banning discussion of certain subjects like politics and religion that always seem to rile people up, but, at least from my expectations of what Foundation staff would be like, research staff should be experienced enough at debating scientific points where they wouldn't get too worked up about it. Does the SCP have an anomalous effect that makes debates grow more heated before an event is triggered? If it does, you may want to make it more clear in the article.

To be clear I'm not trying to convince you to change the containment procedures, just trying to better explain my thoughts on them. Maybe I am missing something on a more cultural level here. I have several who grew up in Mexico and their discussions seem to get livelier and more animated than I'm used to having grown up in American cities and suburbs, so that could be what I'm not really grasping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I made your edits for clinical tone and dating methodology. I decided to keep the containment procedures as is because "attempted mediators will without exception be drawn into the debate," so unless the staff is kept at a maximum of two people (which strikes me as too low) it would be impossible to keep the debate from spreading.

Regarding the sewage, that was honestly inspired by an in-joke — the real-life Wadi Sorek is actually incredibly, disgustingly polluted for most of its run. But in any case the idea is that no filtration system is perfect, so they want to avoid any corpses floating through the populated areas by the coast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the article overall. I like the idea of the historical incident reports. I think those add a great deal to the article. The article has some problems, but I hope you keep working on it because I think it has a unique theme and feel.

The clinical tone lapses in a couple places. The ones that stuck out to me are

approximately 45 meters in length, 20 centimeters wide, and 3.5 meters from base to top.

I'd suggest keep the 'in (dimension)' format all the way through. So: '20 centimeters in width, and 3.5 meters in height.'

it reappears anywhere between 40 and 200 meters away

I'd suggest changing that to something more like 'reappears between 40 and 200 meters distance from its previous location'.

The containment procedures seem a bit strict on discussing the SCP and its properties on site given the requirements to trigger an event. For example:

  • If 2 people are discussing something, there cannot be a minority or a majority.
  • If the debate must grow heated, a simple direction to end debates if one or more participants grow agitated should stop the problem before it occurs. I assume this would work because, as I understand the anomaly, the anomalous properties of the SCP that cause the debate to grow more heated and eventually violent don't occur unless an event is triggered.
  • If the minority is required to feel put upon, standard procedure could be that, in the course of debate and discussion, parties in the debate must make it clear that even though they disagree with other parties, their opinions are valued and respected and no malice is intended in the disagreement.
+ show block
– hide block

This is just from my understanding of the SCP. I could have misinterpreted the description, and if that is the case, please ignore the prior bit of critique.

Another note I have is radio carbon dating doesn't work on something that is currently alive. Radio carbon dating works by measuring the ratio of certain carbon isotopes: a radioactive carbon isotope that decays and 2 carbon isotopes that don't. Since living things are constantly consuming the radioactive carbon isotope (albeit in very small amounts) from their environment, the decaying carbon isotope atoms are constantly being replaced. Organic material can only be carbon dated once it dies and the radioactive carbon isotope ceases to be replenished.

One last note is: I don't understand why the government in the area is pumping sewage into the stream to discourage people from swimming in it. Why not just add that area to the exclusion zone of the site? Especially in the modern day where people are becoming more environmentally conscious, this seems like it would attract more attention than it would dissuade.

Edit: Instead of radio carbon dating, a more plausible measurement of age would be some genetic testing between the anomalous tree and trees of the same type of varying ages in the area to measure genetic drift. I don't know if it would 100% work like that, but again, it's plausible.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy seeing aspects of other cultures explored and showcased on the wiki. This one was quite good… the only aspect I can find to nitpick is the length. It's quite a daunting article at first glance, but after taking the time to read it, I can understand the necessity for it. +1 for bringing an interesting interpretation of Jewish tradition to the table

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I'll do some work on fulfilling that and post it to thecritters again

EDIT 1: Replaced the Nasi and Hyrcanus designations
EDIT 2: Also changed "anomalous strength" to "anomalous abilities. I think the concept of the original majority opinion becoming objectively true is untenable considering the story of Samson is used as an example, but I'm still thinking about this.
EDIT 3: Added a closing note for some more flavor

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an Orthodox Jew, I feel the need to comment here, since, well, I've never seen a more Jewish anomaly than this one.

I might be biased due to my religious background, but I found myself really enjoying the religious references throughout the article and how they tie into the main anomaly. That being said, I can't help but feel dissatisfied at the article's conclusion and unused potential, and as such, am novoting it. However, you're definitely not off the rails here, since this article (with a bit of tweaking) could do very well. Here are my personal recommendations:

  • Try to reduce the amount of formal designations and unnecessary containment procedures in the article. Having too much formality when it's not necessary can bog down an article, and I think that's what's happening here. The SCP-5613-Nasi and SCP-5613-Hyrcanus designations should be disused — majority and minority opinions works just fine here.
  • Work on the clinical tone a little. I think one or two rounds of #thecritters should do the trick, since it's mostly solid as-is.
  • The current "do not allow" condition of the anomaly is that it makes people anomalously strong. Instead of it being as simple as anomalous strength, perhaps tie it into something more philosophical and thematic. My personal idea is that the majority opinion becomes objective truth, but that's just one idea you could explore.
  • The ending needs a little flavor. Somehow, the Foundation discovers the danger of dealing with this anomaly, or perhaps even God… anything could go really. You've got a great premise that has a lot of potential.

Those are my two cents. Hatzlacha on the article! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always up for further suggestions and tweaks! If you have any specific issues, please let me know and I'd be happy to fix it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. You might want to get someone to look at the tone a little, especially in the containment procedures. This feels about one revision away from being truly great.

item.title: Author post yay
item.content:encoded:
Rabbi Natan encountered Elijah and said to him: “What did the Holy Blessed One do at that time?” He said to him: “He smiled and said: My children have triumphed over Me; My children have triumphed over Me!”

Thanks to Uvexar and Dr. Opabinia for their critiques and suggestions!

Image sources (all from Wikimedia Commons):

  • Banner image — CC-BY-4.0
  • Flipped river — CC BY-SA 3.0, edited
  • Carob tree — PD
  • Stone wall — CC BY 2.5
